title: P-Adapters: Robustly Extracting Factual Information from Language Models with Diverse Prompts

structured.summary: This paper introduces P-Adapters, lightweight neural modules that adapt diverse natural language prompts into continuous prompts for frozen large language models, improving the robustness and consistency of factual information extraction without requiring relation or subject annotations at inference. Through extensive experiments on the LAMA dataset and various out-of-distribution settings, P-Adapters demonstrate significant gains in precision and consistency compared to baseline prompting and competitive performance relative to annotation-dependent MoE models.

strengths.point: Novel method enabling annotation-free adaptation of diverse natural language prompts for factual extraction
strengths.evidence: P-Adapters modify prompt embeddings via lightweight modules trained only on (prompt, object) pairs, requiring no relation or subject annotations at inference.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation across multiple LLMs and challenging out-of-distribution scenarios
strengths.evidence: Experiments on BERT Base/Large and RoBERTa Large with OOD prompts, OOD objects, and typographic noise demonstrate robustness improvements.

strengths.point: Insightful analysis identifying the importance of preserving subject entity embeddings
strengths.evidence: Prefix and P-Tuning P-Adapters that preserve subject embeddings outperform Rewrite P-Adapter; interpolation of embeddings further improves performance without annotations.

strengths.point: Clear comparison with MoE models that require annotations, highlighting practical trade-offs
strengths.evidence: MoE models achieve higher precision but need gold relation and subject annotations at inference, whereas P-Adapters operate annotation-free.

strengths.point: Open source release to facilitate reproducibility and further research
strengths.evidence: Authors provide code and training scripts for P-Adapters.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison and discussion of related continuous prompt tuning methods
weaknesses.evidence: No empirical or detailed literature comparison with methods like Prompt Tuning, Prefix Tuning, or AdapterFusion weakens novelty claims.

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablations and justification for architectural choices, especially BiLSTM in Rewrite P-Adapter
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments comparing BiLSTM to simpler or alternative architectures or varying adapter size and depth.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation metrics focusing only on Precision@1 and consistency
weaknesses.evidence: Recall, MRR, Precision@k, or other ranking metrics are omitted, limiting understanding of overall extraction quality.

weaknesses.point: No analysis of robustness to noisy or missing annotations during training
weaknesses.evidence: Real-world data often contains annotation errors, but sensitivity to such noise is not studied.

weaknesses.point: MoE baselines are oracle models using gold annotations at inference, making comparisons less fair
weaknesses.evidence: MoE models require relation and subject annotations at inference, unlike P-Adapters.

weaknesses.point: Unclear how subject entity embeddings are identified and preserved at inference without annotations
weaknesses.evidence: The method depends on preserving subject embeddings, but no explanation is given on how subjects are located in natural language prompts.

weaknesses.point: No qualitative error analysis or failure case discussion
weaknesses.evidence: Lack of examples or analysis limits understanding of when and why P-Adapters fail.

weaknesses.point: Experiments limited to English and relatively small LLMs
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation on multilingual data or larger, more recent LLMs like GPT-3 or PaLM.

weaknesses.point: Sparse details on training hyperparameters, stability, and variance across seeds
weaknesses.evidence: Reproducibility is hindered by missing training details and variance reporting.

weaknesses.point: No runtime, computational efficiency, or memory overhead analysis
weaknesses.evidence: Claims of lightweight adapters are not substantiated with empirical efficiency measurements.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on prompt diversity impact and adapter transferability across relations or domains
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments on how prompt complexity or domain shifts affect performance or adapter generalization.

weaknesses.point: No empirical comparison to simple prompt ensembling or majority voting baselines
weaknesses.evidence: Such annotation-free baselines are common and would contextualize P-Adapters’ gains.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient explanation and ablation of interpolation parameter α in embedding mixing
weaknesses.evidence: No analysis of different α values or rationale for fixed α=0.5.

weaknesses.point: Ethical considerations are brief and lack depth regarding potential misuse and mitigation
weaknesses.evidence: Discussion acknowledges risks but does not explore mitigation strategies or broader impacts.

questions.question: How are subject entity embeddings identified and preserved at inference time without annotations?
questions.motivation: Understanding this is critical for practical deployment since the method relies on preserving subject embeddings but only natural language prompts are available.

questions.question: How does P-Adapters’ performance compare to simple prompt ensembling or majority voting over multiple natural language prompts?
questions.motivation: These annotation-free baselines are commonly used and would help contextualize the practical gains of P-Adapters.

questions.question: Can P-Adapters generalize to multilingual or larger, more recent LLMs beyond BERT and RoBERTa?
questions.motivation: Broader applicability is important for real-world usage across languages and model architectures.

questions.question: What is the impact of varying the interpolation parameter α on Rewrite P-Adapter performance?
questions.motivation: Clarifying this design choice could improve understanding and guide future improvements.

questions.question: How robust are P-Adapters to noisy or incomplete annotations during training?
questions.motivation: Real-world data often contains annotation errors; robustness to such noise affects practical utility.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison and discussion of related continuous prompt tuning methods. No empirical or detailed literature comparison with methods like Prompt Tuning, Prefix Tuning, or AdapterFusion weakens novelty claims
critique_points.point_002: Lack of ablations and justification for architectural choices, especially BiLSTM in Rewrite P-Adapter. No experiments comparing BiLSTM to simpler or alternative architectures or varying adapter size and depth
critique_points.point_003: Limited evaluation metrics focusing only on Precision@1 and consistency. Recall, MRR, Precision@k, or other ranking metrics are omitted, limiting understanding of overall extraction quality
critique_points.point_004: No analysis of robustness to noisy or missing annotations during training. Real-world data often contains annotation errors, but sensitivity to such noise is not studied
critique_points.point_005: MoE baselines are oracle models using gold annotations at inference, making comparisons less fair. MoE models require relation and subject annotations at inference, unlike P-Adapters
critique_points.point_006: Unclear how subject entity embeddings are identified and preserved at inference without annotations. The method depends on preserving subject embeddings, but no explanation is given on how subjects are located in natural language prompts
critique_points.point_007: No qualitative error analysis or failure case discussion. Lack of examples or analysis limits understanding of when and why P-Adapters fail
critique_points.point_008: Experiments limited to English and relatively small LLMs. No evaluation on multilingual data or larger, more recent LLMs like GPT-3 or PaLM
critique_points.point_009: Sparse details on training hyperparameters, stability, and variance across seeds. Reproducibility is hindered by missing training details and variance reporting
critique_points.point_010: No runtime, computational efficiency, or memory overhead analysis. Claims of lightweight adapters are not substantiated with empirical efficiency measurements
critique_points.point_011: Limited discussion on prompt diversity impact and adapter transferability across relations or domains. No experiments on how prompt complexity or domain shifts affect performance or adapter generalization
critique_points.point_012: No empirical comparison to simple prompt ensembling or majority voting baselines. Such annotation-free baselines are common and would contextualize P-Adapters’ gains
critique_points.point_013: Insufficient explanation and ablation of interpolation parameter α in embedding mixing. No analysis of different α values or rationale for fixed α=0.5
critique_points.point_014: Ethical considerations are brief and lack depth regarding potential misuse and mitigation. Discussion acknowledges risks but does not explore mitigation strategies or broader impacts
